build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: Story advances as paced dialogue/narration that the player drives forward (clicking or a key reveals the next line) over recognizable story scenes, rather than dumping all text at once or auto-scrolling uncontrollably. The player can read at their own pace and see who is speaking. Strong submissions integrate dialogue with visible characters, scene changes, and storybook framing; a mostly empty screen with a dialogue box should be judged low even if the text advances correctly. Score 0 if there is no readable advancing dialogue system, if text appears all at once with no pacing, or if the player cannot control progression.

At key moments the game presents the player with a real choice among two or more distinct options, the player selects one, and the story visibly continues down the path that choice implies. Different options should produce different authored beats that can be seen in the resulting scene, dialogue, character reaction, unlocked option, or ending path. Score 0 if there are no choices, if choices all lead to identical content, or if selecting an option has no observable effect.

Choices feed persistent state variables (trust, suspicion, collected truths, flags, a relationship/route value) that the game carries forward, and that state changes later content — which lines appear, who confides in the player, which options unlock, or which ending becomes reachable. A later beat must demonstrably depend on an earlier choice, not just the most recent click or a visible counter changing. Score 0 if the story is effectively linear with no remembered state, if choices never affect anything beyond the immediate next line, or if branches re-converge instantly with no lasting consequence.

The full loop is connected without restarting the executable: title -> story begins -> read, examine, and choose through the branching narrative -> reach an ending -> return to title or restart to play again. Every transition is reachable through in-game UI, and reaching an ending halts story progression on a styled result/ending screen. Score 0 if any transition is missing or broken, if the story cannot reach an ending, or if replaying requires relaunching the application.

Scenes support investigation as an actual player action: the player can select or click meaningful parts of the illustrated scene, receive a distinct response, and see that a clue, truth, or observation was recorded. Hotspots should correspond to recognizable objects, props, character details, or environmental features in the scene; generic rectangles, question marks, or abstract click zones over a primitive backdrop should be judged low. Score 0 if scenes cannot be examined, if all scene clicks behave like generic dialogue advancement, or if discoveries are never visibly acknowledged.

The narrative offers genuinely branching paths, not one road with cosmetic detours: across the demos at least two materially different routes/storylines are observable, where different choices lead to substantially different scenes, events, revelations, or route states rather than the same scenes reworded. Variants must play out differently over more than a single immediate text response. Score 0 if the story is a single linear path, if all branches reconverge to identical content, or if branching is only a one-line variation.

The game reaches multiple distinct endings (a subverted happy ending, a grim reckoning, a hidden truth, an escape, etc.) that are meaningfully different in outcome and tied to how the player played. At least two different endings are observable across the demos as authored conclusions reached through different play paths, not just isolated debug scenarios or the same ending with a different title. Score 0 if there is only one possible ending or no real ending.

The world has narrative breadth: it presents several recognizable fairy-tale tales, chapters, or distinct characters with their own voice, situation, and dark secret, so the story has varied material rather than one short scene repeated. Different tales/characters should pose different dilemmas and reveal different truths through playable scenes, not just titles, labels, or short exposition snippets. Score 0 if there is only a single short scene with no breadth of content.

Choices carry real stakes and consequences that the writing makes felt: a decision visibly helps or harms a character, closes off a path, exposes or buries a truth, or shifts who trusts the player, and the consequence is shown in later content. Wrong, risky, or cruel choices should be able to lead to darker outcomes that are visible in the story, not merely stored as a number. Score 0 if choices have no consequences, if every path is equally safe, or if outcomes never reflect what the player chose.

The writing delivers the dark-fairytale subversion the premise promises: familiar storybook openings are turned toward unsettling truths, so the player feels their expectations being inverted rather than reading a straight retelling. The content should read as sustained authored prose with characters, scene texture, escalation, and a turn; a few strong isolated lines should not carry an otherwise thin demo. Score 0 if there is no actual narrative content or only lorem-style placeholder text.

The investigative layer has depth: examinable objects, props, scene details, or characters reveal different narration, clues, or secrets rather than the same generic line. What is found is gathered into the player's knowledge and at least one discovered clue later unlocks or changes a choice, a line of dialogue, or which ending is reachable, so looking closely is rewarded over rushing. Strong submissions make the clue visually grounded in the scene before it becomes text. Score 0 if examined details are flavor that never affect the story, if findings are never recorded, or if discoveries never influence any later choice, line, or ending.

Core reading UI is clear and stable at 1280x720: the dialogue/narration text is legible in a framed box or storybook panel, the current speaker is identifiable, and choice options are clearly presented and distinguishable as selectable. The UI should support a visual-novel scene rather than dominate an otherwise empty screen. Nothing critical overlaps or runs off-screen. Score 0 if dialogue or choices are unreadable, overlapping, or clipped.

Player input produces clear, immediate feedback: advancing a line visibly changes the text, scene, speaker, portrait, clue state, or choice state; hovering or selecting a choice shows a selected/highlighted state; and a made choice is acknowledged before the story moves on. Feedback limited to instant text replacement in the same static layout should be judged low. Score 0 if input produces no visible response, or if it is impossible to tell that a choice registered.

Story structure is legible: the player can tell where they are in the experience — title vs. scene vs. investigation vs. choice vs. ending — and transitions between scenes and into endings are clear rather than abrupt jumps with no framing. Endings read unmistakably as endings with their own authored presentation. Score 0 if scenes, choices, and endings are visually indistinguishable or transitions are confusing.

Overall visual style is coherent and authored across title, scenes, choices, and endings: a consistent dark-storybook art direction with a deliberate palette, typography, and framing. The game should look like a published visual novel, not a debug screen of text. Strong submissions rely on real illustrated or pixel-art treatment; a scene built mainly from ColorRects, circles, silhouettes, and flat primitive shapes should be judged low even if the colors are coherent. Score 0 if the screen is dominated by default Godot widgets, untextured rectangles, or placeholder text.

Backgrounds and characters use real illustrated or pixel-art assets: scenes have illustrated storybook backdrops and characters are shown with sprites/portraits that establish who is present, where the scene takes place, and the mood. Bare colored shapes, circles as character heads, empty backdrops, or simple geometric stand-ins should receive little credit. Score 0 if scenes have no background art and characters are absent or are plain rectangles/circles.

The dialogue box, name plates, choice menus, clue panels, title, and ending screens use styled, themed UI — illustrated frames, ornamented panels, custom or styled fonts, and consistent chrome — fitting the dark-fairytale theme, rather than raw default Godot Label/Button/Panel defaults or plain flat boxes. UI polish should make the game feel like a storybook interface, not just readable text containers. Score 0 if primary UI is obviously unstyled default widgets.

Presentation has motion and polish appropriate to a visual novel: text reveals progressively, scene/character transitions use fades or slides, choices and endings animate in, and there is atmosphere (subtle character or background motion, mood lighting, particles, or an animated title) rather than instant hard cuts everywhere. Motion should support the mood beyond text appearing in a box. Score 0 if nothing on screen ever animates and every change is an instant snap.
